entries.response:
**Purpose:** `templates/pas.md` defines conventions, patterns, and documentation for authoring PAS `.dot` pipeline files — the primary workflow definition format for the project.

## Pipeline File Structure

Pipelines are `.dot` files in `pipelines/`. Required digraph attributes: `label` (human name), `goal` (objective, reference beads issue IDs), `model` (default LLM). Exact one `Mdiamond` start node, one `Msquare` exit node.

## Node Types

| Shape | `node_type` | Behavior |
|-------|-------------|----------|
| `box` | `task` (default) | Runs Claude Code with `prompt` attribute |
| `diamond` | `conditional` | Claude outputs label to route edges |
| `hexagon` | `human_gate` | Pauses for approval |
| `parallelogram` | `tool_node` | Runs shell command via `tool_command` |

## Edge Routing

Conditional edges require `label` (Claude output value) and `condition="preferred_label=<LABEL>"`. Example at `pipelines/fix-sync-partial-failure.dot`.

## Node Attributes

`prompt` (required for box), `llm_model` (override per-node), `allowed_tools` (restrict to Read/Grep/Glob for investigation), `max_budget_usd` (spending cap), `goal_gate=true` (pipeline gate), `retry_target` (loop destination on gate failure).

## Standard 6-Phase Pattern

```
start → investigate → implement → write_tests → run_tests → verify → close_issue → done
                                                                    └─ FAIL → fixup → verify (loop)
```

- `investigate`: read-only (`allowed_tools="Read,Grep,Glob"`), write findings to `.pas/`
- `implement`: specific file paths in prompt, no prior context in `-p` mode
- `write_tests`: match existing test patterns
- `run_tests`: include exact test command (e.g. `uv run pytest tests/ -k sync`)
- `verify`: diamond conditional, output PASS or FAIL
- `fixup`: only on FAIL, loop to verify
- `close_issue`: `allowed_tools="Bash(bd:*),Bash(git:*)"`

## Prompt Rules

- One concern per node, no combining
- Be specific: include file paths, function names, exact commands
- Write output files to `.pas/` if generating reports
- Reference beads issue in `goal` attribute
- Include exact test commands in prompt, not "run tests"

## Planning Workflow (PRD → Spec → Beads → Pipeline)

```bash
pas plan --prd --from-prompt "..."
pas plan --spec --from-prompt "..."
pas generate spec.md prd.md    # Generate pipeline from spec
pas decompose .pas/spec.md    # Decompose into beads epic
pas scaffold <EPIC_ID>       # Scaffold pipeline from beads
pas run pipelines/<EPIC_ID>.dot -w .
pas run templates/plan-to-execute.dot -w .  # Meta-pipeline
```

## CLI Commands

```bash
pas validate pipelines/my-pipeline.dot   # Validate before running
pas run pipelines/my-pipeline.dot -w .  # Run with working directory
pas info pipelines/my-pipeline.dot      # Inspect structure
```

## Key Conventions

- Node names (e.g. `investigate`, `implement`, `verify`) must match the standard pattern
- Always `allowed_tools="Read,Grep,Glob"` for read-only phases
- Use exact commands in prompts, never vague instructions
- Reference beads issue IDs in `goal` attribute for every pipeline

2. [Secondary goal - supporting outcome]

- [What's explicitly out of scope for this work]

- [Technical details, API signatures, configuration options]

[Code examples or configuration snippets]
```

- [Description and technical details]

- [Continue numbering for additional requirements]

- Platform or language version requirements
- Performance targets or resource limits
- Dependencies on other systems or services
- Security or compliance requirements

## Risks & Mitigations

| Risk | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|--------|------------|
| [Description of potential risk] | [High/Medium/Low - what breaks if this happens] | [Specific actions to prevent or handle the risk] |

- [Related work that should be separate initiatives]
- [Known limitations to address in future iterations]

- [Prior art or similar implementations]

For beads integration:

- Link this PRD to the epic ID in the metadata

-->

**Purpose statement here.**

**templates/prd-template.md defines the canonical PRD template for this project, establishing documentation structure, numbering conventions, and workflow rules for product requirements.**

## Template Structure

- Metadata header: Status, Author, Created, Beads Epic fields
- Overview, Goals, Non-Goals, User Stories (US-N), Functional Requirements (FR-N), Technical Constraints, Success Criteria, Risks & Mitigations, Out of Scope, References

## Numbering Conventions

- `US-N` — user stories (US-1, US-2, US-3...)
- `FR-N` — functional requirements (FR-1, FR-2, FR-3...)

## Workflow Rules

1. Replace all `[bracketed placeholders]` before use
2. Update Status: `Draft` → `In Review` → `Approved`
3. Link PRD to beads epic via `Beads Epic: [epic-id]` header
4. Omit sections that don't apply (template is optional-flexible)
5. Add custom sections as needed

## Beads Integration Rules

- Create epic via `bd create --type=epic`
- Break FRs into individual beads tasks
- Track blocking via dependencies
- Use epic ID in metadata header

## Success Criteria Mandate

Must be **objective and testable** — avoid subjective criteria; include specific metrics, verification methods, performance targets.

## Reproduction-Critical Content

None — this is a documentation template, not logic or string constants.

## Annex References

None.

File: docs/web-interface-plan.md

```markdown
# PAS Web Interface — Implementation Plan

## Context

PAS (Pascal's Discrete Attractor) is a DOT-based pipeline runner for AI workflows. You're building a parallel workflow on another branch that generates PRDs and spec files, decomposes them into beads, and creates `.dot` pipeline files. This web interface provides a visual layer for that planning process — a place to prompt, review, iterate on PRD/spec documents, and then kick off pipeline execution with live streaming progress.

The repo is a git worktree of the main PAS project on `feature/interface-web`.

## Architecture

**Leptos fullstack app** — single Rust crate that compiles to both server (Axum) and client (WASM). Uses `cargo-leptos` as the build tool.

### New Crate: `attractor-web`

```
crates/attractor-web/
├── Cargo.toml
├── src/
│   ├── lib.rs                    # App definition, hydrate entry
│   ├── main.rs                   # Server binary (Axum)
│   ├── app.rs                    # Router: /, /editor, /execute
│   ├── state.rs                  # AppState (sessions, broadcast channels)
│   ├── pages/
│   │   ├── mod.rs
│   │   ├── prompt.rs             # Chat input → LLM generates PRD/spec
│   │   ├── editor.rs             # Two-pane markdown editor (PRD | Spec)
│   │   └── execution.rs          # Pipeline execution with live streaming
│   ├── components/
│   │   ├── mod.rs
│   │   ├── chat_input.rs         # Multi-line prompt input
│   │   ├── markdown_pane.rs      # Textarea + live preview
│   │   └── node_status.rs        # Pipeline node progress indicator
│   └── server/
│       ├── mod.rs
│       ├── generate.rs           # Server fn: prompt → PRD + spec via claude CLI
│       ├── execute.rs            # Server fn: start pipeline, return session_id
│       ├── stream.rs             # SSE endpoint: bridge PipelineEvents to browser
│       └── session.rs            # Session tracking, checkpoint/resume
├── style/
│   └── main.scss
└── public/
```

### Data Flow

```
1. User types prompt at /
2. Server calls: claude -p "<system+user prompt>" --output-format stream-json
   → Streams partial content back to browser via server fn
   → Accumulates final result, splits into PRD + Spec sections
3. Navigate to /editor with PRD (left pane) and Spec (right pane)
4. User edits both documents freely, iterates
5. User clicks "Execute" → server fn:
   - Generates session_id (UUID v4)
   - Writes PRD + Spec to workdir/.pas/
   - Spawns pipeline via PipelineExecutor with EventEmitter
   - Returns session_id
6. Browser opens EventSource to /api/stream/{session_id}
   → EventEmitter.subscribe() → SSE events to browser
   → ExecutionPage shows node progress, streaming output, cost
```

### Streaming: Two Layers

**Layer 1: Claude CLI → Server** (for PRD/spec generation)
- `claude -p "..." --output-format stream-json --include-partial-messages`
- Read stdout line-by-line as NDJSON
- Each line has `type` field: `system`, `assistant` (text deltas), `result`
- Stream text deltas to browser as they arrive

**Layer 2: Pipeline Events → Browser** (for execution)
- The existing `EventEmitter` (broadcast channel) in `events.rs` already emits `PipelineEvent` variants: `StageStarted`, `StageCompleted`, `EdgeSelected`, etc.
- SSE endpoint subscribes to the emitter and forwards events as JSON
- Browser `EventSource` receives and renders them

### Modifications to Existing Crates

**`attractor-pipeline/src/handlers/mod.rs`** — CodergenHandler changes:
- Add `CodergenConfig` to support `--output-format stream-json` + `--session-id`
- When `stream-json` mode: read stdout line-by-line, parse NDJSON, forward content deltas to an optional `broadcast::Sender`, accumulate final `result` for Outcome
- When `json` mode (default): unchanged behavior, backward compatible
- Add `--session-id <uuid>` flag instead of `--no-session-persistence` when a session_id is provided

**`attractor-pipeline/src/checkpoint.rs`** — Add `session_id: Option<String>` to `PipelineCheckpoint` for resume across checkpoint/restore cycles.

**`attractor-pipeline/src/engine.rs`** — Accept optional `EventEmitter` in `PipelineExecutor` (may already be wired; the web crate will construct one and subscribe).

### Key Dependencies

```toml
leptos = "0.7"
leptos_axum = "0.7"
leptos_router = "0.7"
leptos_meta = "0.7"
axum = "0.8"
pulldown-cmark = "0.12"     # Markdown rendering (compiles to WASM)
tokio-stream = "0.1"        # BroadcastStream for SSE
# Plus workspace deps: tokio, serde, serde_json, uuid, tracing
# Plus internal: attractor-types, attractor-pipeline, attractor-dot (server-only)
```

### Session ID & Checkpoint/Resume

1. `start_pipeline()` generates a UUID v4 session_id
2. Stored in `AppState.sessions: HashMap<String, SessionState>`
3. `SessionState` holds: session_id, EventEmitter, pipeline status, checkpoint path
4. CodergenHandler passes `--session-id <uuid>` to claude CLI (instead of `--no-session-persistence`)
5. If browser disconnects and reconnects to `/api/stream/{session_id}`, it re-subscribes to the existing broadcast channel
6. If the server restarts, `load_checkpoint()` restores state and the same session_id resumes the claude conversation

## Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: Scaffold (get pixels on screen)
- Add `crates/attractor-web` to workspace
- Set up Leptos + Axum with `cargo-leptos`
- Three routes with placeholder content: `/`, `/editor`, `/execute`
- `ChatInput` component on `/`
- Two side-by-side `MarkdownPane` components on `/editor` (hardcoded content)
- Verify `cargo leptos watch` works

### Phase 2: PRD/Spec Generation
- `generate_prd_spec` server function in `server/generate.rs`
- Shell out to `claude -p` with a system prompt that produces structured PRD + Spec
- Start with `--output-format json` (simpler; streaming comes in Phase 3)
- Wire prompt page → server fn → navigate to editor with results

### Phase 3: Streaming Infrastructure
- Modify `CodergenHandler` with `CodergenConfig` for `stream-json` support
- Add `session_id` to `PipelineCheckpoint`
- SSE endpoint at `/api/stream/{session_id}`
- Upgrade PRD generation to use `stream-json` for live typing effect

### Phase 4: Pipeline Execution
- `start_pipeline` server function
- Wire "Execute" button → start pipeline → open SSE stream
- `ExecutionPage` renders node progress, content, cost in real time

### Phase 5: Resume & Polish
- Session resume on browser reconnect
- Checkpoint-based resume on server restart
- Styling, loading states, error handling

## Verification

1. `cargo leptos watch` — app builds and renders at localhost:3000
2. Type a prompt → PRD + Spec appear in two-pane editor
3. Edit both documents, click Execute
4. Execution page shows live node progress via SSE
5. Disconnect browser, reconnect → stream resumes
6. `cargo test --workspace` — all existing tests still pass

```

Lead with a single bold purpose statement: **[FileName] does X.**
Then use ## headings to organize the remaining content.
Every file MUST include at minimum:
- A purpose statement (first line, bold)
- Exported symbols with signatures (under any appropriate heading)
Choose additional sections based on file content.

entries.response:
**docs/web-interface-plan.md** defines the implementation plan for `attractor-web`, a Leptos fullstack app providing a visual UI layer for PAS pipeline workflows.

## Architecture

- **Leptos + Axum** fullstack via `cargo-leptos`; compiles to WASM client + server binary
- Server module exports: `generate_prd_spec` (claude CLI → PRD+Spec), `start_pipeline` (spawn executor, return session_id), `stream_sse` (SSE endpoint `/api/stream/{session_id}`), `session.rs` (checkpoint/resume tracking)
- Three routes: `/` (prompt), `/editor` (two-pane markdown), `/execute` (live pipeline progress)
- Components: `ChatInput`, `MarkdownPane`, `NodeStatus`

## Data Flow

1. User prompt → `claude -p` → split into PRD/Spec → `/editor`
2. User edits → "Execute" → generate UUID v4 session_id → write to workdir/.pas/ → `PipelineExecutor` spawns with `EventEmitter` → returns session_id
3. Browser connects `EventSource` to `/api/stream/{session_id}` → receives `PipelineEvent` variants (StageStarted, StageCompleted, EdgeSelected) as JSON via SSE

## Streaming Layers

- **Layer 1 (Claude→Server)**: `claude -p "..." --output-format stream-json --include-partial-messages`; parse NDJSON line-by-line, stream `assistant` text deltas to browser, accumulate final `result`
- **Layer 2 (Pipeline→Browser)**: `EventEmitter` (broadcast channel) → SSE endpoint → browser EventSource

## Required Modifications to Existing Crates

- **CodegenHandler** in `attractor-pipeline/src/handlers/mod.rs`: Add `CodegenConfig` struct with `stream_json: bool`, optional `broadcast::Sender`; read stdout as NDJSON when streaming, forward content deltas, accumulate `Outcome.result`; replace `--no-session-persistence` with `--session-id <uuid>` when session_id provided
- **PipelineCheckpoint** in `checkpoint.rs`: Add `session_id: Option<String>` field
- **PipelineExecutor** in `engine.rs`: Accept optional `EventEmitter` parameter

## Session Management

- `AppState.sessions: HashMap<String, SessionState>` holds session_id, EventEmitter, pipeline status, checkpoint path
- Browser disconnect/reconnect re-subscribes to existing broadcast channel
- Server restart triggers `load_checkpoint()` to restore state and resume same session_id

## Key Dependencies

```toml
leptos = "0.7", leptos_axum = "0.7", leptos_router = "0.7", leptos_meta = "0.7"
axum = "0.8", pulldown-cmark = "0.12", tokio-stream = "0.1"
# workspace deps: tokio, serde, serde_json, uuid, tracing
# server-only: attractor-types, attractor-pipeline, attractor-dot
```

## Implementation Phases

1. **Scaffold**: Add crate to workspace, `cargo-leptos` setup, three placeholder routes, `ChatInput` + two `MarkdownPane` components
2. **PRD/Spec Generation**: `generate.rs` server fn, `claude -p` with `--output-format json`, navigate to editor with results
3. **Streaming Infrastructure**: `CodegenConfig` for stream-json, `session_id` in checkpoint, SSE endpoint, live typing effect
4. **Pipeline Execution**: `start_pipeline` → SSE stream → `ExecutionPage` renders node progress, content, cost
5. **Resume & Polish**: Browser reconnect, server restart resume, styling

## Verification Criteria

- `cargo leptos watch` renders at localhost:3000
- Prompt → PRD+Spec in two-pane editor
- Execute → live node progress via SSE
- Reconnect browser → stream resumes
- `cargo test --workspace` passes (existing tests unaffected)

entries.prompt:
Analyze this source file and generate a summary that captures what an AI coding assistant needs to know to work with this file effectively.

File: templates/plan-to-execute.dot

```text
digraph PlanToExecute {
    label="Plan to Execute: Generate PRD → Spec → Beads Tasks → Pipeline"
    goal="Transform a feature description into a complete, validated pipeline with beads task tracking."
    model="sonnet"

    start [shape="Mdiamond"]
    done  [shape="Msquare"]

    // --- Generate PRD ---
    generate_prd [
        shape="box"
        label="Generate PRD"
        allowed_tools="Write"
        prompt="Read the feature_description from context.

Using the PRD template structure (see templates/prd-template.md):
- Title and metadata (Status: Draft, Author, Created date)
- Overview (1-2 paragraphs)
- Goals (numbered list)
- Non-Goals
- User Stories (US-N format)
- Functional Requirements (FR-N format with code examples)
- Technical Constraints
- Success Criteria (numbered, measurable)
- Risks & Mitigations (table format)
- Out of Scope
- References

Write the complete PRD to .pas/prd.md in markdown format."
    ]

    // --- Review PRD ---
    review_prd [
        shape="hexagon"
        label="Review PRD"
        prompt="Review the generated PRD at .pas/prd.md

Does the PRD clearly capture:
- What problem we're solving?
- Why it matters now?
- Specific goals and success criteria?
- User stories with measurable outcomes?
- Technical requirements and constraints?

Respond with 'continue' to proceed to spec generation, or 'reject' to regenerate the PRD."
    ]

    // --- Generate Spec ---
    generate_spec [
        shape="box"
        label="Generate Spec"
        allowed_tools="Read,Write"
        prompt="Read the PRD from .pas/prd.md

Using the Technical Specification template structure (see templates/spec-template.md):
- Title and metadata (Status: Draft, PRD link)
- Architecture Overview (high-level design, before/after)
- File Changes (numbered list of files/components with before/after code)
- Implementation Phases (Phase N with checkbox tasks and dependencies)
  * CRITICAL: This section drives the beads decompose step
  * Structure: ### Phase N: [Title] with - [ ] tasks listed
- Configuration (environment variables, settings table)
- Testing Strategy (unit, integration, E2E, manual tests)
- Rollback Plan (steps to revert, verification)

Write the complete spec to .pas/spec.md in markdown format."
    ]

    // --- Review Spec ---
    review_spec [
        shape="hexagon"
        label="Review Spec"
        prompt="Review the generated Technical Specification at .pas/spec.md

Does the spec clearly describe:
- How the feature will be architecturally implemented?
- What files will be changed and why?
- Specific implementation phases with clear tasks?
- Configuration and testing requirements?
- How to roll back if problems occur?

Respond with 'continue' to proceed to task decomposition, or 'reject' to regenerate the spec."
    ]

    // --- Decompose to Beads ---
    decompose [
        shape="box"
        label="Decompose to Beads"
        allowed_tools="Read,Write,Bash(bd:*)"
        prompt="Read the spec from .pas/spec.md

Parse the Implementation Phases section and create beads tasks:
1. Create a beads epic: bd create --type=epic --title='[Feature Name] Epic' --priority=2
   Copy the epic ID
2. For each Phase in the spec:
   - Create a phase epic: bd create --type=epic --title='Phase N: [Title]' --priority=2
   - Add dependency: bd dep add <phase-epic> <parent-epic>
3. For each task in each Phase:
   - Create beads task: bd create --type=task --title='[Task description]' --priority=2
   - Add dependency: bd dep add <task-id> <phase-epic>
4. Write the root epic ID to .pas/epic_id.txt
5. Sync beads: bd sync"
    ]

    // --- Scaffold Pipeline ---
    scaffold [
        shape="parallelogram"
        label="Scaffold Pipeline"
        allowed_tools="Read,Write,Bash(sed:*)"
        prompt="Read the epic ID from .pas/epic_id.txt

Use sed to replace EPIC_ID in the epic-runner template:
sed 's/EPIC_ID/<epic-id>/g' templates/epic-runner.dot > .pas/pipeline.dot

This creates a ready-to-run pipeline that executes all beads tasks."
    ]

    // --- Validate Pipeline ---
    validate_pipeline [
        shape="parallelogram"
        label="Validate Pipeline"
        allowed_tools="Bash(pas:*)"
        prompt="Run: pas validate .pas/pipeline.dot

Check for errors:
- Missing nodes
- Invalid shapes
- Unresolved edge labels
- Type mismatches

If valid, proceed to execution. If invalid, fix the pipeline or respond with FAIL to regenerate."
    ]

    // --- Execute Pipeline ---
    execute_pipeline [
        shape="parallelogram"
        label="Execute Pipeline"
        allowed_tools="Bash(pas:*),Bash(bd:*)"
        prompt="Run: pas run .pas/pipeline.dot -w .

This executes the full pipeline:
- Picks next ready task
- Investigates changes needed
- Implements the changes
- Runs tests
- Verifies completion
- Loops until all tasks are done

Monitor output and respond with status updates."
    ]

    // --- Edges ---
    start -> generate_prd

    generate_prd -> review_prd

    review_prd -> generate_prd [label="reject", condition="preferred_label=reject"]
    review_prd -> generate_spec [label="continue"]

    generate_spec -> review_spec

    review_spec -> generate_spec [label="reject", condition="preferred_label=reject"]
    review_spec -> decompose [label="continue"]

    decompose -> scaffold

    scaffold -> validate_pipeline

    validate_pipeline -> scaffold [label="fail", condition="preferred_label=FAIL"]
    validate_pipeline -> execute_pipeline [label="pass"]

    execute_pipeline -> done
}

```

Lead with a single bold purpose statement: **[FileName] does X.**
Then use ## headings to organize the remaining content.
Every file MUST include at minimum:
- A purpose statement (first line, bold)
- Exported symbols with signatures (under any appropriate heading)
Choose additional sections based on file content.

entries.response:
**Purpose:** Templates the "Plan to Execute" workflow: Generate PRD → Review PRD → Generate Spec → Review Spec → Decompose to Beads → Scaffold Pipeline → Validate Pipeline → Execute Pipeline.

## Overview

DOT graph defining a 9-node pipeline workflow with review gates and conditional branching. Each node contains `allowed_tools` and `prompt` attributes controlling execution behavior.

## Node Structure

| Node | Shape | Tools | Purpose |
|------|-------|-------|---------|
| `start` | Mdiamond | — | Workflow entry point |
| `generate_prd` | box | Write | Writes PRD to `.pas/prd.md` from feature description |
| `review_prd` | hexagon | — | Reviews PRD completeness, responds with `continue` or `reject` |
| `generate_spec` | box | Read,Write | Writes spec to `.pas/spec.md` from PRD |
| `review_spec` | hexagon | — | Reviews spec quality, responds with `continue` or `reject` |
| `decompose` | box | Read,Write,Bash(bd:*) | Creates beads epic, phase epics, and tasks from spec |
| `scaffold` | parallelogram | Read,Write,Bash(sed:*) | Replaces EPIC_ID in template, outputs `.pas/pipeline.dot` |
| `validate_pipeline` | parallelogram | Bash(pas:*) | Runs `pas validate .pas/pipeline.dot`, returns `pass` or `fail` |
| `execute_pipeline` | parallelogram | Bash(pas:*),Bash(bd:*) | Runs `pas run .pas/pipeline.dot -w .` |
| `done` | Msquare | — | Workflow completion |

## Edge Conditions

```dot
review_prd -> generate_prd [label="reject", condition="preferred_label=reject"]
review_prd -> generate_spec [label="continue"]
review_spec -> generate_spec [label="reject", condition="preferred_label=reject"]
review_spec -> decompose [label="continue"]
validate_pipeline -> scaffold [label="fail", condition="preferred_label=FAIL"]
validate_pipeline -> execute_pipeline [label="pass"]
```

Condition matching: `preferred_label` must equal `reject`, `continue`, `FAIL`, or `pass`.

## Conventions

- Hexagon nodes: review gates with human-in-the-loop decisions
- Parallelogram nodes: automated pipeline operations
- Box nodes: LLM-driven content generation
- Files use `.pas/` prefix for pipeline artifacts
- Review gates enforce PRD/spec quality before proceeding
- Beads decompose step requires `Implementation Phases` section in spec

```markdown
# Technical Specification: [Feature or Epic Title]

**Status:** Draft | In Review | Approved
**Author:** [Your Name]
**Created:** [YYYY-MM-DD]
**PRD Link:** [Link to corresponding PRD or epic]

---

## Architecture Overview

[Provide a high-level architectural description of how the feature will be implemented. Include:
- System components and their relationships
- Data flow and interactions
- Key design decisions and rationale
- Before/after diagrams or descriptions if helpful]

### Before/After (Optional)

**Before:**
[Current state or behavior]

**After:**
[New state or behavior after implementation]

---

## File Changes

[List each file or component that will be modified. Use numbered sections for each file.]

### 1. [Component/File Name]

**Location:** `path/to/file.rs` or `path/to/module/`

**Change Description:**
[Describe what will change in this file and why]

**Before:**
```
[Current code or structure]
```

**After:**
```
[New code or structure]
```

---

### 2. [Another Component/File Name]

**Location:** `path/to/another/file.ext`

**Change Description:**
[Describe the modification]

**Before:**
```
[Current implementation]
```

**After:**
```
[New implementation]
```

---

## Implementation Phases

[This section is critical - the `decompose` CLI command reads these phases to create beads tasks. Structure each phase with clear task lists and dependencies.]

### Phase 1: [Title]

**Description:** [What this phase accomplishes]

**Tasks:**
- [ ] [T1-1] Task description with estimated scope
- [ ] [T1-2] Subtask or related work
- [ ] [T1-3] Complete this phase requirement

**Dependencies:** [Other phases or tasks that must be completed first]

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [Verifiable criterion for phase completion]
- [Test or validation requirement]

---

### Phase 2: [Title]

**Description:** [What this phase accomplishes]

**Tasks:**
- [ ] [T2-1] Task description
- [ ] [T2-2] Related implementation work
- [ ] [T2-3] Integration or testing tasks

**Dependencies:** Phase 1 (must complete Phase 1 before starting this phase)

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [Criterion for phase completion]
- [Verification or test requirement]

---

### Phase 3: [Title]

**Description:** [What this phase accomplishes]

**Tasks:**
- [ ] [T3-1] Final implementation tasks
- [ ] [T3-2] Verification and validation
- [ ] [T3-3] Documentation or cleanup

**Dependencies:** Phase 2

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [Final acceptance criterion]
- [Complete implementation check]

---

## Configuration

[Document any environment variables, settings, configuration files, or prerequisites needed.]

### Environment Variables

| Variable | Type | Required | Description |
|----------|------|----------|-------------|
| `VAR_NAME` | string | Yes | [What this variable controls] |
| `ANOTHER_VAR` | integer | No | [Optional configuration, default value] |

### Prerequisites

| Item | Description | How to Setup |
|------|-------------|--------------|
| [Dependency Name] | [What it is] | [Steps to install/configure] |
| [Tool or Library] | [Purpose in implementation] | [Installation or setup command] |

---

## Testing Strategy

[Describe how the implementation will be tested. Include different testing approaches.]

### 1. Unit Tests

[Describe unit tests for individual functions/modules]
- Test coverage targets: [percentage or specific modules]
- Mock dependencies: [What will be mocked]
- Test file location: `tests/` or `src/`

### 2. Integration Tests

[Describe integration tests for component interactions]
- Component combinations to test
- Setup and teardown requirements
- Expected behavior verification

### 3. End-to-End Tests

[Describe E2E tests for complete features]
- User workflows to validate
- Test data and scenarios
- Performance or load testing if applicable

### 4. Manual Testing Checklist

- [ ] [Manually test feature X]
- [ ] [Verify behavior in condition Y]
- [ ] [Check compatibility with existing features]
- [ ] [Validate error handling scenarios]

---

## Rollback Plan

[Describe how to revert these changes if problems occur in production.]

### Rollback Steps

1. [First step to revert - e.g., revert commit or feature flag]
2. [Stop/restart services if needed]
3. [Restore database state or data migrations]
4. [Verify rollback completed successfully]

### Rollback Verification

- [ ] [Check that system is in prior state]
- [ ] [Verify no data loss or corruption]
- [ ] [Confirm performance is back to baseline]
- [ ] [Validate no side effects remain]

### Known Limitations

[Document any limitations or risks in rollback:
- Irreversible changes (database migrations that can't be reversed)
- Manual intervention required
- Data considerations during rollback]

---

## Notes

[Any additional context, decisions, or considerations that don't fit other sections:]

- [Design decision and rationale]
- [Architectural alternative considered and why it wasn't chosen]
- [Known technical debt or future improvements]
- [References to spike/investigation work done during planning]

---

<!--
INSTRUCTIONS FOR USING THIS TEMPLATE:

1. Replace all [bracketed placeholders] with actual content
2. Update Status as you progress (Draft → In Review → Approved)
3. Link to the PRD that defines the what/why for this spec
4. Make Architecture Overview clear enough for anyone to understand the design
5. Be specific in File Changes with actual code examples
6. Implementation Phases MUST have clear task lists - this is what `decompose` reads
7. Include realistic dependencies between phases
8. Configuration section should be comprehensive and include examples
9. Testing Strategy should cover all critical paths and edge cases
10. Rollback Plan must be concrete and testable

For decompose integration:
- Create a PRD linked in the metadata
- Structure Implementation Phases with numbered tasks [T-Phase-Task format]
- List clear dependencies between phases
- Run `bd decompose <spec>` to generate beads tasks from phases
- Each phase becomes a beads epic, each task becomes a beads task
- Dependencies translate to beads blocking relationships

Tips:
- Keep phases logically grouped around deliverables
- Make task descriptions small enough to complete in one session
- Include acceptance criteria for each phase so you know when it's done
- Reference this spec in beads issues for full traceability
-->

```

Lead with a single bold purpose statement: **[FileName] does X.**
Then use ## headings to organize the remaining content.
Every file MUST include at minimum:
- A purpose statement (first line, bold)
- Exported symbols with signatures (under any appropriate heading)
Choose additional sections based on file content.
entries.systemPrompt:
You are analyzing source code to generate documentation for AI coding assistants.

TASK:
Analyze the file and produce a dense, identifier-rich summary. Choose the documentation topics most relevant to THIS specific file. Do not follow a fixed template — adapt your sections to what matters most.

Consider topics such as (choose what applies):
- What this file IS (its role in the project)
- Public interface: exported functions, classes, types, constants with signatures
- Key algorithms, data structures, or state management
- Integration points and coupling with other modules
- Configuration, environment, or runtime requirements
- Error handling strategies or validation boundaries
- Concurrency, lifecycle, or resource management concerns
- Domain-specific patterns (middleware chains, event handlers, schema definitions, factories)
- Behavioral contracts: verbatim regex patterns, format strings, output templates, magic constants, sentinel values, error code strings, environment variable names
- Workflow & convention rules: if this file defines contribution guidelines, PR conventions, commit standards, testing mandates, tool usage requirements, approval workflows, AI agent behavioral instructions, or code conventions (naming standards, formatting rules, import ordering, linting policies), extract them as explicit, actionable rules. Sources: CONTRIBUTING.md, CI configs, PR templates, style guides, linter/formatter configs (.eslintrc, .prettierrc, .editorconfig), tsconfig strictness, README dev sections.


TARGET LENGTH (MANDATORY):
- Source file: 6347 characters
- Target summary: ~1587 characters (25% compression)
- Maximum: 1904 characters
- Achieve this by: ultra-dense writing, minimal examples, single-sentence descriptions

ULTRA-COMPRESSION TECHNIQUES:
- Use telegraphic style: "exports foo, bar, baz" not "this file exports three functions"
- No code examples unless critical to understanding
- One sentence per export maximum
- Omit obvious parameter names ("opts: Options" not "options: Options - configuration object")
- Use abbreviations: "params" not "parameters", "config" not "configuration"
- Skip section if empty (don't write "No special concerns" or "N/A")

DENSITY RULES (MANDATORY):
- Every sentence must reference at least one specific identifier (function name, class name, type name, or constant)
- Never use filler phrases: "this file", "this module", "provides", "responsible for", "is used to", "basically", "essentially", "provides functionality for"
- Use the pattern: "[ExportName] does X" not "The ExportName function is responsible for doing X"
- Use technical shorthand: "exports X, Y, Z" not "this module exports a function called X..."
- Compress descriptions: "parses YAML frontmatter from .sum files" not "responsible for the parsing of YAML-style frontmatter..."

ANCHOR TERM PRESERVATION (MANDATORY):
- All exported function/class/type/const names MUST appear in the summary exactly as written in source
- Key parameter types and return types MUST be mentioned
- Preserve exact casing of identifiers (e.g., buildAgentsMd, not "build agents md")
- Missing any exported identifier is a failure

WHAT TO INCLUDE:
- All exported function/class/type/const names
- Parameter types and return types for public functions
- Key dependencies and what they're used for
- Notable design patterns (name them explicitly: "Strategy pattern", "Builder pattern", etc.)
- Only critical TODOs (security, breaking issues)

WHAT TO EXCLUDE:
- Control flow minutiae (loop structures, variable naming, temporary state)
- Generic descriptions without identifiers
- Filler phrases and transitions

BEHAVIORAL CONTRACTS (NEVER EXCLUDE):
- Regex patterns for parsing/validation/extraction — include the full pattern verbatim in backticks
- Format strings, output templates, serialization structures — show exact format
- Magic constants, sentinel values, numeric thresholds (timeouts, buffer sizes, retry counts)
- Prompt text or template strings that control AI/LLM behavior
- Error message patterns and error code strings used for matching
- Environment variable names and their expected values
- File format specifications (YAML frontmatter schemas, NDJSON line formats)
These define observable behavior that must be reproduced exactly.

WORKFLOW & CONVENTION RULES (NEVER EXCLUDE):
- Contribution requirements: branch naming, commit message formats, PR description templates
- Testing mandates: required test types, coverage thresholds, "never delete tests" policies
- Tool usage rules: required CLI commands, browser automation tools, linter/formatter requirements
- Approval workflows: review requirements, CI checks that must pass, deployment gates
- AI agent instructions: explicit directives for automated assistants (coding rules, prohibited actions)
- Code conventions: naming standards (camelCase, PascalCase, snake_case for what), import ordering rules, file/folder naming patterns, formatting rules (indentation, quotes, semicolons), language idioms, TypeScript strictness policies, preferred patterns vs anti-patterns
These define how contributors (human or AI) must work within the project.

REPRODUCTION-CRITICAL CONTENT (ANNEX OVERFLOW):
Some files exist primarily to define large string constants, prompt templates,
configuration arrays, default value sets, or command/IDE template content.
For these files:
- Write a CONCISE summary following the standard density and length rules
- List each constant/export by name with a one-line description of its role
- Do NOT attempt to reproduce multi-line string constants verbatim in the summary
- Instead, end the summary with a dedicated ## Annex References section listing
  each reproduction-critical constant:

  ## Annex References
  - `FILE_SYSTEM_PROMPT` — system prompt for file analysis (250 lines)
  - `DIRECTORY_SYSTEM_PROMPT` — system prompt for AGENTS.md generation (150 lines)

  The pipeline will extract the actual constant values from source code and write
  them to a companion .annex.sum file automatically. Your job is to IDENTIFY which
  constants are reproduction-critical, not to reproduce them inline.

For files that are primarily logic (functions, classes, algorithms), ignore this
section — it does not apply.

OUTPUT FORMAT (MANDATORY):
- Start your response DIRECTLY with the purpose statement — a single bold line: **Purpose statement here.**
- Do NOT include any preamble, thinking, or meta-commentary before the purpose statement
- Do NOT say "Here is...", "Now I'll...", "Based on my analysis...", "Let me create...", "Perfect."
- Your response IS the documentation — not a message about the documentation
entries.response:
**Purpose statement here.**
**Purpose statement here.**

**spec-template.md defines the standardized format for writing Technical Specifications that integrate with the `decompose` CLI and `beads` task system.**

## Workflow & Convention Rules

- **Status lifecycle:** `Draft → In Review → Approved` — update Status field as spec progresses
- **PRD linkage required:** Every spec must link to the PRD that defines the what/why in the `PRD Link:` field
- **Implementation Phases** structure is **read by `decompose` CLI** — malformed phases break `bd decompose <spec>` generation
- **Task ID format:** `[T-Phase-Task]` (e.g., `T1-1`, `T2-3`) — each task becomes a beads task; phases become beads epics
- **Dependencies** field in each phase translates to beads **blocking relationships**
- **Acceptance Criteria** required per phase — verifiable completion conditions
- **File Changes** section uses numbered subsections with `Before:`/`After:` code blocks

## `decompose` CLI Integration

- `decompose` reads Implementation Phases to auto-generate beads tasks
- Each `### Phase N` becomes a beads epic
- Each `- [ ] [T-N-X] Task` becomes a beads task with ID `T-N-X`
- `bd decompose <spec>` parses the spec and creates corresponding beads issues

## Required Sections

| Section | Purpose | Notes |
|---------|---------|-------|
| `Architecture Overview` | System design, components, data flow | Optional Before/After subsection |
| `File Changes` | Per-file modifications with Before/After code | Numbered subsections |
| `Implementation Phases` | **Critical for `decompose`** — phased task lists with Dependencies and Acceptance Criteria | Must use `[T-Phase-Task]` ID format |
| `Configuration` | Environment variables, prerequisites | Tables with Variable/Type/Required/Description columns |
| `Testing Strategy` | Unit, Integration, E2E, Manual checklist | Coverage targets, mock dependencies |
| `Rollback Plan` | Concrete rollback steps, verification checklist, Known Limitations | Must be testable |

## Template Conventions

- Author fills all `[bracketed placeholders]`
- Code blocks in File Changes use triple-backtick fencing
- Phases should be logically grouped around deliverables
- Task descriptions should be completable in one session
- Reference spec in beads issues for traceability

CASCADE, in dependency order): DROP TABLE IF EXISTS outreach_engagement_events CASCADE; DROP TABLE IF EXISTS outreach_delivery_attempts CASCADE; DROP TABLE IF EXISTS outreach_messages CASCADE; DROP TABLE IF EXISTS inbox_messages CASCADE; DROP TABLE IF EXISTS outreach_item_events CASCADE; DROP TABLE IF EXISTS outreach_items CASCADE; DROP TABLE IF EXISTS outreach_drafts CASCADE; DROP TABLE IF EXISTS outreach_logs CASCADE; DROP TABLE IF EXISTS outreach_experiments CASCADE; DROP TABLE IF EXISTS outreach_suppression_list CASCADE; DROP TABLE IF EXISTS audit_requests CASCADE; DROP TABLE IF EXISTS lead_events CASCADE; DROP TABLE IF EXISTS lead_competitors CASCADE; DROP TABLE IF EXISTS leads CASCADE; DROP TABLE IF EXISTS free_report_confirmations CASCADE; DROP TABLE IF EXISTS app_state_snapshots CASCADE; SECTION 2 — CREATE the 7 new tables in dependency order: TABLE 1 relationships — id text PRIMARY KEY, company_id text NOT NULL, company_name text NOT NULL, primary_contact_email text, primary_contact_name text, domain text, city text, niche text, relationship_stage text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'target', active_workflow_instance_id text (no FK yet — circular dependency resolved in section 3), owner_user_id text, next_action text, next_action_at timestamptz, waiting_on text, blocking_reason text, memory_summary text, context jsonb NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}'::jsonb, last_meaningful_event_at timestamptz, closed_reason text, version integer NOT NULL DEFAULT 1, created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(), updated_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(). Indexes: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_workflow_instances_one_active ON workflow_instances (relationship_id) WHERE is_active = true; CREATE INDEX idx_workflow_instances_type_state ON workflow_instances (workflow_type, state, next_action_at); CREATE INDEX idx_workflow_instances_owner ON workflow_instances (owner_user_id, state, due_at) WHERE owner_user_id IS NOT NULL; CREATE INDEX idx_workflow_instances_relationship ON workflow_instances (relationship_id, is_active). TABLE 3 workflow_tasks — id text PRIMARY KEY, workflow_instance_id text NOT NULL REFERENCES workflow_instances(id), task_type text NOT NULL, status text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending', assignee_user_id text, priority integer NOT NULL DEFAULT 100, due_at timestamptz, started_at timestamptz, completed_at timestamptz, blocked_reason text, payload jsonb NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}'::jsonb, artifact_refs jsonb NOT NULL DEFAULT '[]'::jsonb, created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(), updated_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(). Indexes: CREATE INDEX idx_workflow_tasks_instance_status ON workflow_tasks (workflow_instance_id, status, due_at); CREATE INDEX idx_workflow_tasks_assignee ON workflow_tasks (assignee_user_id, status, due_at) WHERE assignee_user_id IS NOT NULL; CREATE INDEX idx_workflow_tasks_type_status ON workflow_tasks (task_type, status). TABLE 4 workflow_events — id text PRIMARY KEY, relationship_id text NOT NULL REFERENCES relationships(id), workflow_instance_id text REFERENCES workflow_instances(id), workflow_task_id text REFERENCES workflow_tasks(id), event_type text NOT NULL, from_state text, to_state text, actor_type text NOT NULL, actor_id text, reason_code text, correlation_id text, metadata jsonb NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}'::jsonb, occurred_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(), created_row_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(). Indexes: CREATE INDEX idx_workflow_events_relationship ON workflow_events (relationship_id, occurred_at DESC); CREATE INDEX idx_workflow_events_instance ON workflow_events (workflow_instance_id, occurred_at DESC) WHERE workflow_instance_id IS NOT NULL; CREATE INDEX idx_workflow_events_type ON workflow_events (event_type, occurred_at DESC); CREATE INDEX idx_workflow_events_correlation ON workflow_events (correlation_id) WHERE correlation_id IS NOT NULL. TABLE 5 communication_artifacts — id text PRIMARY KEY, relationship_id text NOT NULL REFERENCES relationships(id), workflow_instance_id text REFERENCES workflow_instances(id), workflow_task_id text REFERENCES workflow_tasks(id), artifact_type text NOT NULL, provider text, provider_id text, thread_id text, direction text, status text, summary text, payload jsonb NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}'::jsonb, occurred_at timestamptz, created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(), updated_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(). Indexes: CREATE INDEX idx_artifacts_relationship ON communication_artifacts (relationship_id, artifact_type, created_at DESC); CREATE INDEX idx_artifacts_workflow ON communication_artifacts (workflow_instance_id, artifact_type) WHERE workflow_instance_id IS NOT NULL; CREATE INDEX idx_artifacts_thread ON communication_artifacts (thread_id) WHERE thread_id IS NOT NULL; CREATE INDEX idx_artifacts_provider ON communication_artifacts (provider, provider_id) WHERE provider_id IS NOT NULL. TABLE 6 suppressions — id text PRIMARY KEY, relationship_id text NOT NULL REFERENCES relationships(id), scope text NOT NULL, suppression_type text NOT NULL, status text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'active', starts_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(), ends_at timestamptz, reason text, metadata jsonb NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}'::jsonb, created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(), updated_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(). Indexes: CREATE INDEX idx_suppressions_relationship ON suppressions (relationship_id, status); CREATE INDEX idx_suppressions_active ON suppressions (status, suppression_type) WHERE status = 'active'; CREATE INDEX idx_suppressions_expiring ON suppressions (ends_at) WHERE ends_at IS NOT NULL AND status = 'active'. TABLE 7 verification_findings — id text PRIMARY KEY, relationship_id text REFERENCES relationships(id), workflow_instance_id text REFERENCES workflow_instances(id), workflow_task_id text REFERENCES workflow_tasks(id), finding_type text NOT NULL, severity text NOT NULL, status text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'open', summary text NOT NULL, details jsonb NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}'::jsonb, detected_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(), resolved_at timestamptz, created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(), updated_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(). Indexes: CREATE INDEX idx_findings_status ON verification_findings (status, severity); CREATE INDEX idx_findings_relationship ON verification_findings (relationship_id) WHERE relationship_id IS NOT NULL; CREATE INDEX idx_findings_type ON verification_findings (finding_type, status). SECTION 3 — Add circular FK: ALTER TABLE relationships ADD CONSTRAINT fk_relationships_active_workflow FOREIGN KEY (active_workflow_instance_id) REFERENCES workflow_instances(id);"
    ]

    verify_migration [
        label="Migration runs clean?"
        shape="diamond"
        timeout=10m
        node_type="conditional"
        allowed_tools="Read,Bash"
        goal_gate=true
        prompt="Verify that sql/migrations/0007_eleanor_workflow_schema.sql executes without errors. Steps: (1) Check the DATABASE_URL environment variable is set. (2) Run the migration: psql $DATABASE_URL -f sql/migrations/0007_eleanor_workflow_schema.sql. Capture the exit code and output. (3) If successful, verify the 7 new tables exist by running: psql $DATABASE_URL -c \"SELECT table_name FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema='public' ORDER BY table_name;\" and confirm relationships, workflow_instances, workflow_tasks, workflow_events, communication_artifacts, suppressions, verification_findings are all present. (4) Verify legacy tables are gone: leads, outreach_items, outreach_drafts, outreach_messages, inbox_messages, outreach_delivery_attempts, outreach_engagement_events, outreach_item_events, outreach_logs, outreach_experiments, outreach_suppression_list, audit_requests, lead_events, lead_competitors, app_state_snapshots should NOT appear in the table list. (5) Verify non-workflow tables are intact: brands, prompts, scan_runs, report_runs, org_subscriptions should still be present. (6) Verify the unique partial index on workflow_instances exists: psql $DATABASE_URL -c \"SELECT indexname FROM pg_indexes WHERE tablename='workflow_instances' AND indexname='idx_workflow_instances_one_active';\" (7) Verify the circular FK was added: psql $DATABASE_URL -c \"SELECT constraint_name FROM information_schema.table_constraints WHERE table_name='relationships' AND constraint_type='FOREIGN KEY';\" and confirm fk_relationships_active_workflow is present. Emit PASS if migration exits 0, all 7 tables exist, all legacy tables are gone, and non-workflow tables are intact. Emit FAIL with specific error details otherwise."
    ]

    fix_migration [
        label="Fix migration errors"
        shape="box"
        timeout=15m
        allowed_tools="Read,Write,Bash"
        prompt="Fix errors in sql/migrations/0007_eleanor_workflow_schema.sql based on the psql error output. Common issues: (1) FK creation order error — relationships must be created BEFORE workflow_instances but WITHOUT the active_workflow_instance_id FK (which creates the circular dependency). The FK must be added via ALTER TABLE after both tables exist. Check that the DROP and CREATE order is correct. (2) 'table does not exist' on DROP — ensure all DROP statements use IF EXISTS. (3) jsonb default syntax — must be '{}'::jsonb and '[]'::jsonb with explicit cast. (4) Partial index WHERE clause syntax — must be a simple column condition e.g. WHERE is_active = true, WHERE owner_user_id IS NOT NULL. (5) Constraint name conflicts from a previous failed run — add DROP CONSTRAINT IF EXISTS before adding constraints, or wrap in a DO $ block. (6) Transaction aborted mid-way — fix the error that caused the abort, then the whole BEGIN/COMMIT block will retry clean. Do not remove the BEGIN/COMMIT wrapper."
    ]

    write_seed [
        label="Write seed.ts"
        shape="box"
        timeout=20m
        allowed_tools="Read,Write,Glob,Bash"
        prompt="Create the file apps/api/src/workflow/seed.ts. First, read existing database client files to find how the project connects to PostgreSQL (check apps/api/src/db.ts, apps/api/src/database.ts, or similar). Also read apps/api/src/workflow/types.ts to confirm the genId import path. The seed script must export an async function seedWorkflow() and include a main guard (if require.main === module or if import.meta.url === ...) to call it. The function must insert 3 relationships, 3 workflow instances, 3 workflow tasks, and 6 workflow events in the following order to avoid FK violations: STEP 1 — Insert 3 relationships with active_workflow_instance_id = NULL: (a) Acme Corp: id=genId('rel_'), company_id='company_acme', company_name='Acme Corp', domain='acmecorp.com', relationship_stage='attempting_contact', version=1. (b) Widget Inc: id=genId('rel_'), company_id='company_widget', company_name='Widget Inc', domain='widgetinc.com', relationship_stage='nurturing', version=1. (c) TechStart LLC: id=genId('rel_'), company_id='company_techstart', company_name='TechStart LLC', domain='techstart.io', relationship_stage='implementing', version=1. STEP 2 — Insert 3 workflow instances: (a) Acme cold_outreach: id=genId('wfi_'), relationship_id=acme.id, workflow_type='cold_outreach', workflow_version=1, state='in_progress', substate='draft_needed', is_active=true, version=1. (b) Widget nurture: id=genId('wfi_'), relationship_id=widget.id, workflow_type='nurture', workflow_version=1, state='scheduled', substate='parked_until_signal', is_active=true, next_action_at=30 days from now, version=1. (c) TechStart implementation: id=genId('wfi_'), relationship_id=techstart.id, workflow_type='implementation', workflow_version=1, state='in_progress', substate='setup_in_progress', is_active=true, version=1. STEP 3 — Insert 3 workflow tasks: (a) Acme task: id=genId('wft_'), workflow_instance_id=acmeWfi.id, task_type='generate_draft', status='ready', priority=100. (b) Widget task: id=genId('wft_'), workflow_instance_id=widgetWfi.id, task_type='generate_nurture_content', status='pending', priority=100, due_at=30 days from now. (c) TechStart task: id=genId('wft_'), workflow_instance_id=techstartWfi.id, task_type='deliver_baseline_report', status='in_progress', priority=100, started_at=now. STEP 4 — Insert 6 workflow events (relationship_created and workflow_started for each): For each relationship emit a relationship_created event (actor_type='system', event_type='relationship_created'), and for each workflow instance emit a workflow_started event (actor_type='system', event_type='workflow_started', from_state=null, to_state='in_progress' or 'scheduled' as appropriate). All event IDs use genId('wfe_'). STEP 5 — UPDATE relationships to set active_workflow_instance_id: UPDATE relationships SET active_workflow_instance_id=acmeWfi.id WHERE id=acme.id; same for widget and techstart. Use the project's db client to run all inserts. If the project uses a query builder (Drizzle, Kysely) use it; if raw SQL use parameterized queries. Log 'Seed complete' on success. Handle errors by logging and re-throwing."
    ]

    verify_seed [
        label="Seed + canonical queries pass?"
        shape="diamond"
        timeout=10m
        node_type="conditional"
        allowed_tools="Read,Bash"
        goal_gate=true
        prompt="Verify the seed script runs and the five canonical queries return correct results. (1) Run the seed script: cd apps/api && npx tsx src/workflow/seed.ts (or ts-node, whichever the project uses). Verify it exits 0. (2) Verify row counts: psql $DATABASE_URL -c 'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM relationships;' — expect 3. psql $DATABASE_URL -c 'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM workflow_instances;' — expect 3. psql $DATABASE_URL -c 'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM workflow_tasks;' — expect 3. psql $DATABASE_URL -c 'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM workflow_events;' — expect 6. (3) Run the five canonical queries for Acme Corp: Q1 (stage): SELECT relationship_stage FROM relationships WHERE company_name='Acme Corp' — expect 'attempting_contact'. Q2 (active workflow): SELECT workflow_type, state FROM workflow_instances WHERE relationship_id=(SELECT id FROM relationships WHERE company_name='Acme Corp') AND is_active=true — expect cold_outreach / in_progress. Q3 (next action): SELECT task_type, status FROM workflow_tasks WHERE workflow_instance_id=(SELECT id FROM workflow_instances WHERE relationship_id=(SELECT id FROM relationships WHERE company_name='Acme Corp') AND is_active=true) AND status IN ('pending','ready') — expect generate_draft / ready. Q4 (owner): SELECT owner_user_id FROM workflow_instances WHERE relationship_id=(SELECT id FROM relationships WHERE company_name='Acme Corp') AND is_active=true — may be null, that is acceptable. Q5 (due): SELECT next_action_at FROM workflow_instances WHERE relationship_id=(SELECT id FROM relationships WHERE company_name='Acme Corp') AND is_active=true — return value noted. (4) Verify active_workflow_instance_id back-pointer is set: SELECT active_workflow_instance_id FROM relationships WHERE company_name='Acme Corp' — must NOT be null. Emit PASS if seed exits 0, row counts are correct, Q1-Q3 return expected values, and back-pointers are set. Emit FAIL with specific details otherwise."
    ]

    fix_seed [
        label="Fix seed errors"
        shape="box"
        timeout=15m
        allowed_tools="Read,Write,Bash,Glob"
        prompt="Fix errors in apps/api/src/workflow/seed.ts based on the error output from running tsx or ts-node. Common issues: (1) FK violation on relationships insert — active_workflow_instance_id must be NULL on initial insert, then UPDATEd after workflow_instances are created. Confirm STEP 5 UPDATE is present and runs after all inserts. (2) Wrong db client import — re-read existing service files (apps/api/src/) to find the correct import. (3) genId import path wrong — re-check apps/api/src/workflow/types.ts export. (4) Canonical query Q1 returns wrong stage — check that relationship_stage is being set correctly in the INSERT. (5) Canonical query Q2 returns no rows — check that is_active=true is being set on workflow instance inserts. (6) Row count wrong — check for duplicate inserts if seed was run twice; add a cleanup block at the start: DELETE FROM workflow_events WHERE relationship_id IN (SELECT id FROM relationships WHERE company_name IN ('Acme Corp','Widget Inc','TechStart LLC')); and similarly for other tables, in reverse FK order, then re-insert. Make targeted fixes and do not break working inserts."
    ]

    verify_non_workflow [
        label="Non-workflow tables intact?"
        shape="diamond"
        timeout=10m
        node_type="conditional"
        allowed_tools="Bash,Read"
        goal_gate=true
        prompt="Verify that all non-workflow tables are intact and queryable after the migration. Run: psql $DATABASE_URL -c \"SELECT table_name FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema='public' ORDER BY table_name;\" and check that each of the following tables appears in the output: brands, brand_competitors, prompts, configured_models, scan_runs, scan_entries, scan_entry_mentions, report_runs, report_details, report_artifacts, report_events, optimization_actions, optimization_action_events, optimization_kpi_snapshots, cta_funnel_events, org_subscriptions, model_config_snapshots, contact_verification_cache. Also spot-check data integrity: run SELECT COUNT(*) FROM brands; and SELECT COUNT(*) FROM scan_runs; — if either returns an error or unexpectedly zero rows (and the project is not brand new), flag it. Run SELECT COUNT(*) FROM configured_models; — should return without error. Also verify Better Auth tables exist: user, session, account, organization. Emit PASS if all non-workflow tables are present and SELECT COUNT(*) runs without error on brands, scan_runs, and configured_models. Emit FAIL if any table is missing with the specific table names that are absent."
    ]

    fix_collateral [
        label="Fix collateral damage"
        shape="box"
        timeout=15m
        allowed_tools="Read,Write,Bash"
        prompt="Fix any non-workflow tables that were accidentally dropped or damaged by the migration in sql/migrations/0007_eleanor_workflow_schema.sql. First, identify which tables are missing by diffing the expected table list against psql output. Then: (1) If the migration has an overly broad DROP statement that matched a non-workflow table — edit the migration file to be more specific and add IF EXISTS guards. (2) If a non-workflow table is missing and a pg_dump backup exists — restore it with: pg_restore -d $DATABASE_URL --table=<table_name> --data-only backup_YYYYMMDD.dump. (3) If no backup exists and the table structure is known from the codebase — reconstruct the CREATE TABLE statement from ORM schema files or migration history and re-create it. Document what was lost and what was fixed. After fixing, note that the migration file must be edited so the issue does not recur."
    ]

    commit_changes [
        label="Commit changes"
        shape="box"
        timeout=10m
        allowed_tools="Bash"
        prompt="Commit all Phase 1 changes to git. Run: git add apps/api/src/workflow/types.ts sql/migrations/0007_eleanor_workflow_schema.sql apps/api/src/workflow/seed.ts && git commit -m 'feat(eleanor): Phase 1 — schema, types, migration, and seed data\n\nCreate 7 canonical workflow tables replacing 15+ legacy tables.\nAdd TypeScript types, enums, and row interfaces.\nAdd seed script with 3 sample relationships.\nAll canonical queries verified against seed data.'"
    ]

    done [
        label="Done"
        shape="Msquare"
        prompt="Phase 1 complete. Deliverables produced: (1) apps/api/src/workflow/types.ts — all TypeScript enums, const arrays, union types, row interfaces, and genId helper, compiling with no errors. (2) sql/migrations/0007_eleanor_workflow_schema.sql — migration that drops 15 legacy tables and creates 7 canonical tables (relationships, workflow_instances, workflow_tasks, workflow_events, communication_artifacts, suppressions, verification_findings) with all indexes and the circular FK between relationships and workflow_instances. (3) apps/api/src/workflow/seed.ts — seed script creating 3 sample relationships in different stages with active workflows and tasks. All five canonical queries verified against seed data. Non-workflow tables (brands, scans, reports, auth, billing) are unaffected."
    ]

    start -> write_types

    write_types -> verify_types

    verify_types -> write_migration [label="PASS" condition="preferred_label=PASS"]
    verify_types -> fix_types [label="FAIL", condition="preferred_label=FAIL"]
    fix_types -> verify_types [loop_restart=true]

    write_migration -> verify_migration

    verify_migration -> write_seed [label="PASS" condition="preferred_label=PASS"]
    verify_migration -> fix_migration [label="FAIL", condition="preferred_label=FAIL"]
    fix_migration -> verify_migration [loop_restart=true]

    write_seed -> verify_seed

    verify_seed -> verify_non_workflow [label="PASS" condition="preferred_label=PASS"]
    verify_seed -> fix_seed [label="FAIL", condition="preferred_label=FAIL"]
    fix_seed -> verify_seed [loop_restart=true]

    verify_non_workflow -> commit_changes [label="PASS" condition="preferred_label=PASS"]
    commit_changes -> done
    verify_non_workflow -> fix_collateral [label="FAIL", condition="preferred_label=FAIL"]
    fix_collateral -> verify_non_workflow [loop_restart=true]
}
```

entries.systemPrompt:
You are analyzing source code to generate documentation for AI coding assistants.

TASK:
Analyze the file and produce a dense, identifier-rich summary. Choose the documentation topics most relevant to THIS specific file. Do not follow a fixed template — adapt your sections to what matters most.

Consider topics such as (choose what applies):
- What this file IS (its role in the project)
- Public interface: exported functions, classes, types, constants with signatures
- Key algorithms, data structures, or state management
- Integration points and coupling with other modules
- Configuration, environment, or runtime requirements
- Error handling strategies or validation boundaries
- Concurrency, lifecycle, or resource management concerns
- Domain-specific patterns (middleware chains, event handlers, schema definitions, factories)
- Behavioral contracts: verbatim regex patterns, format strings, output templates, magic constants, sentinel values, error code strings, environment variable names
- Workflow & convention rules: if this file defines contribution guidelines, PR conventions, commit standards, testing mandates, tool usage requirements, approval workflows, AI agent behavioral instructions, or code conventions (naming standards, formatting rules, import ordering, linting policies), extract them as explicit, actionable rules. Sources: CONTRIBUTING.md, CI configs, PR templates, style guides, linter/formatter configs (.eslintrc, .prettierrc, .editorconfig), tsconfig strictness, README dev sections.


TARGET LENGTH (MANDATORY):
- Source file: 33643 characters
- Target summary: ~8411 characters (25% compression)
- Maximum: 10093 characters
- Achieve this by: ultra-dense writing, minimal examples, single-sentence descriptions

ULTRA-COMPRESSION TECHNIQUES:
- Use telegraphic style: "exports foo, bar, baz" not "this file exports three functions"
- No code examples unless critical to understanding
- One sentence per export maximum
- Omit obvious parameter names ("opts: Options" not "options: Options - configuration object")
- Use abbreviations: "params" not "parameters", "config" not "configuration"
- Skip section if empty (don't write "No special concerns" or "N/A")

DENSITY RULES (MANDATORY):
- Every sentence must reference at least one specific identifier (function name, class name, type name, or constant)
- Never use filler phrases: "this file", "this module", "provides", "responsible for", "is used to", "basically", "essentially", "provides functionality for"
- Use the pattern: "[ExportName] does X" not "The ExportName function is responsible for doing X"
- Use technical shorthand: "exports X, Y, Z" not "this module exports a function called X..."
- Compress descriptions: "parses YAML frontmatter from .sum files" not "responsible for the parsing of YAML-style frontmatter..."

ANCHOR TERM PRESERVATION (MANDATORY):
- All exported function/class/type/const names MUST appear in the summary exactly as written in source
- Key parameter types and return types MUST be mentioned
- Preserve exact casing of identifiers (e.g., buildAgentsMd, not "build agents md")
- Missing any exported identifier is a failure

WHAT TO INCLUDE:
- All exported function/class/type/const names
- Parameter types and return types for public functions
- Key dependencies and what they're used for
- Notable design patterns (name them explicitly: "Strategy pattern", "Builder pattern", etc.)
- Only critical TODOs (security, breaking issues)

WHAT TO EXCLUDE:
- Control flow minutiae (loop structures, variable naming, temporary state)
- Generic descriptions without identifiers
- Filler phrases and transitions

BEHAVIORAL CONTRACTS (NEVER EXCLUDE):
- Regex patterns for parsing/validation/extraction — include the full pattern verbatim in backticks
- Format strings, output templates, serialization structures — show exact format
- Magic constants, sentinel values, numeric thresholds (timeouts, buffer sizes, retry counts)
- Prompt text or template strings that control AI/LLM behavior
- Error message patterns and error code strings used for matching
- Environment variable names and their expected values
- File format specifications (YAML frontmatter schemas, NDJSON line formats)
These define observable behavior that must be reproduced exactly.

WORKFLOW & CONVENTION RULES (NEVER EXCLUDE):
- Contribution requirements: branch naming, commit message formats, PR description templates
- Testing mandates: required test types, coverage thresholds, "never delete tests" policies
- Tool usage rules: required CLI commands, browser automation tools, linter/formatter requirements
- Approval workflows: review requirements, CI checks that must pass, deployment gates
- AI agent instructions: explicit directives for automated assistants (coding rules, prohibited actions)
- Code conventions: naming standards (camelCase, PascalCase, snake_case for what), import ordering rules, file/folder naming patterns, formatting rules (indentation, quotes, semicolons), language idioms, TypeScript strictness policies, preferred patterns vs anti-patterns
These define how contributors (human or AI) must work within the project.

REPRODUCTION-CRITICAL CONTENT (ANNEX OVERFLOW):
Some files exist primarily to define large string constants, prompt templates,
configuration arrays, default value sets, or command/IDE template content.
For these files:
- Write a CONCISE summary following the standard density and length rules
- List each constant/export by name with a one-line description of its role
- Do NOT attempt to reproduce multi-line string constants verbatim in the summary
- Instead, end the summary with a dedicated ## Annex References section listing
  each reproduction-critical constant:

  ## Annex References
  - `FILE_SYSTEM_PROMPT` — system prompt for file analysis (250 lines)
  - `DIRECTORY_SYSTEM_PROMPT` — system prompt for AGENTS.md generation (150 lines)

  The pipeline will extract the actual constant values from source code and write
  them to a companion .annex.sum file automatically. Your job is to IDENTIFY which
  constants are reproduction-critical, not to reproduce them inline.

For files that are primarily logic (functions, classes, algorithms), ignore this
section — it does not apply.

OUTPUT FORMAT (MANDATORY):
- Start your response DIRECTLY with the purpose statement — a single bold line: **Purpose statement here.**
- Do NOT include any preamble, thinking, or meta-commentary before the purpose statement
- Do NOT say "Here is...", "Now I'll...", "Based on my analysis...", "Let me create...", "Perfect."
- Your response IS the documentation — not a message about the documentation
entries.response:
**Purpose: Define Phase 1 pipeline for Eleanor workflow engine schema migration, type generation, and seed data creation.**

## Overview

The file defines a DOT digraph representing Phase 1 of the Eleanor AI workflow engine — replacing 15+ legacy scattered workflow tables with 7 canonical tables. The pipeline uses goal gates with PASS/FAIL condition routing between verification nodes and fix nodes.

## Nodes

| Node | Type | Timeout | Role |
|------|------|---------|------|
| `start` | Mdiamond | — | Entry point |
| `write_types` | box | 20m | Generate `apps/api/src/workflow/types.ts` |
| `verify_types` | diamond (goal_gate) | 10m | TypeScript compilation check |
| `fix_types` | box | 15m | Fix compilation errors |
| `write_migration` | box | 20m | Generate `sql/migrations/0007_eleanor_workflow_schema.sql` |
| `verify_migration` | diamond (goal_gate) | 10m | Migration execution check |
| `fix_migration` | box | 15m | Fix migration errors |
| `write_seed` | box | 20m | Generate `apps/api/src/workflow/seed.ts` |
| `verify_seed` | diamond (goal_gate) | 10m | Seed + canonical queries check |
| `fix_seed` | box | 15m | Fix seed errors |
| `verify_non_workflow` | diamond (goal_gate) | 10m | Non-workflow tables integrity |
| `fix_collateral` | box | 15m | Fix collateral damage |
| `commit_changes` | box | 10m | Git commit |
| `done` | Msquare | — | Phase 1 complete |

## Edge Routing

All verification nodes use `condition="preferred_label=PASS"` for success path, `condition="preferred_label=FAIL"` for failure path. Fix nodes loop back via `[loop_restart=true]`.

## Deliverables

1. **`apps/api/src/workflow/types.ts`** — TypeScript enums, const arrays with `as const`, union types, row interfaces, `genId` helper with prefixes `rel_`, `wfi_`, `wft_`, `wfe_`, `art_`, `sup_`, `vrf_`

2. **`sql/migrations/0007_eleanor_schema.sql`** — Drops 15 legacy tables (outreach_items, leads, inbox_messages, etc.), creates 7 canonical tables with indexes and circular FK

3. **`apps/api/src/workflow/seed.ts`** — Inserts 3 relationships (Acme Corp, Widget Inc, TechStart LLC), 3 workflow instances, 3 tasks, 6 events, then UPDATEs back-pointers

## Allowed Tools Pattern

Each node specifies permitted tools via `allowed_tools` attribute — typically `Read,Write,Glob,Bash` for generation steps; `Read,Bash` for verification steps.

## Verification Criteria

- **verify_types**: `tsc --noEmit` exits 0
- **verify_migration**: psql runs clean, 7 tables exist, legacy tables gone, circular FK added
- **verify_seed**: Row counts (3/3/3/6), five canonical queries return expected values, back-pointers set
- **verify_non_workflow**: Non-workflow tables (brands, scan_runs, configured_models, Better Auth tables) intact

## Workflow State Enum

`WorkflowState`: not_started, ready, in_progress, waiting_on_prospect, waiting_on_rosco, scheduled, blocked, completed, stopped — with TERMINAL states (completed, stopped).

```text
digraph BuildBeadsIntegration {
    label="Build Beads Integration for Attractor CLI"

Look at the BLOCKS section to see child tasks and their statuses.

If all child tasks are closed (no more ready tasks for this epic):

        shape="box"

Run bd show <task-id> to get full details.

This is a Rust project at /Volumes/qwiizlab/projects/attractor with this structure:
  crates/attractor-cli/src/main.rs     — CLI binary (clap commands: Run, Validate, Info)
  crates/attractor-pipeline/src/       — Pipeline engine, handlers, validation
    handler.rs                         — NodeHandler trait, HandlerRegistry, default_registry()

  crates/attractor-types/              — Context, Outcome, error types
  templates/                           — Template files (attractor.md, beads.md)
  docs/examples/epic-runner.dot        — Epic runner pipeline template

Investigate the codebase to understand what needs to change for this specific task:
- Find the relevant files, functions, and existing patterns
- Understand the current code structure
- Identify exactly what needs to change

Write your findings to .pas/investigation.md including:
- Files to modify (with full paths)
- Current behavior/code to change
- Required changes with specifics
- Existing patterns to follow
- Any risks or dependencies"
    ]

    // =========================================================================
    // Implement — make the changes
    // =========================================================================
    implement [
        shape="box"
        label="Implement"
        prompt="Read .pas/current_task.md for the task and .pas/investigation.md for the plan.

Implement the changes described. This is a Rust project — follow existing code patterns and conventions.

Key conventions:
- CLI commands use clap derives in crates/attractor-cli/src/main.rs
- Handler registration happens in default_registry() in crates/attractor-pipeline/src/handler.rs
- Templates go in the templates/ directory at the project root
- DOT pipeline files follow Graphviz digraph syntax with attractor-specific attributes
- Use async_trait for handler implementations
- Re-exports go in crates/attractor-pipeline/src/lib.rs

For template files (markdown, DOT): create them directly with proper content.
For Rust code: follow the existing patterns in the file you're modifying.

Keep changes minimal and focused on the task requirements."
    ]

    // =========================================================================
    // Build & test — compile and run tests
    // =========================================================================
    run_tests [
        shape="box"
        label="Build & Test"
        prompt="Read .pas/current_task.md to understand what was changed.

Run the build and test suite:
1. cargo build --workspace  (must compile cleanly)
2. cargo test -p attractor-pipeline  (existing tests must pass)
3. cargo test -p attractor-dot  (parser tests must pass)
4. If the task added a new CLI command, run: cargo run -p attractor-cli -- --help  (verify it shows up)
5. If the task created a DOT file, run: cargo run -p attractor-cli -- validate <path-to-dot-file>  (must validate)
6. If the task created a template file, verify it exists and has content

If tests or build fail, fix the issues and re-run until green.
Write the results to .pas/test-results.md"
    ]

    // =========================================================================
    // Verify — review the changes
    // =========================================================================
    verify [
        shape="diamond"
        label="Verify"
        node_type="conditional"
        prompt="Read .pas/current_task.md for the task requirements.
Check the changes:
1. Review the git diff for this task's changes
2. Verify cargo build --workspace succeeds
3. Verify cargo test --workspace succeeds
4. Read .pas/test-results.md for test outcomes
5. Check that the task's description requirements are fully met

If everything looks good, respond with PASS on the last line.
If there are problems, respond with FAIL on the last line."
    ]

    // =========================================================================
    // Fixup — address verification failures
    // =========================================================================
    fixup [
        shape="box"
        label="Fix Issues"
        prompt="Read .pas/current_task.md and .pas/investigation.md for context.
The verification step found problems. Fix any:
- Compilation errors
- Failing tests
- Missing functionality from the task description
- Lint or formatting issues

Re-run cargo build --workspace && cargo test --workspace after fixing."
    ]

    // =========================================================================
    // Close this task — commit and mark done
    // =========================================================================
    close_task [
        shape="box"
        label="Close Task"
        allowed_tools="Bash(bd:*),Bash(git:*)"
        prompt="Read .pas/current_task.md to get the task ID.
1. Stage the relevant changed files (be specific, don't use git add -A):
   - For template tasks: git add templates/<filename>
   - For CLI tasks: git add crates/attractor-cli/src/main.rs
   - For handler tasks: git add crates/attractor-pipeline/src/handler.rs crates/attractor-pipeline/src/lib.rs
   - For DOT files: git add pipelines/<filename> or templates/<filename>
2. Commit with a descriptive message referencing the task ID, e.g.:
   git commit -m 'Add PRD template (attractor-dcu)'
3. Close the task: bd close <task-id> --reason='Implemented successfully'
4. Run: bd sync --flush-only"
    ]

    // =========================================================================
    // Check if more tasks remain in the epic
    // =========================================================================
    check_remaining [
        shape="diamond"
        label="More Tasks?"
        node_type="conditional"
        llm_model="haiku"
        allowed_tools="Bash(bd:*)"
        prompt="Run: bd show attractor-asr
Look at the BLOCKS section. Count how many child tasks are still open.

Run: bd ready
Check if any remaining child tasks of epic attractor-asr are ready (unblocked, open).

If there are more ready tasks, respond with MORE on the last line.
If all tasks are closed or remaining tasks are blocked, respond with DONE on the last line."
    ]

    // =========================================================================
    // Edges
    // =========================================================================
    start -> pick_task

    pick_task -> investigate [label="MORE", condition="preferred_label=MORE"]
    pick_task -> done        [label="DONE", condition="preferred_label=DONE"]

    investigate -> implement -> run_tests -> verify

    verify -> close_task [label="PASS", condition="preferred_label=PASS"]
    verify -> fixup      [label="FAIL", condition="preferred_label=FAIL"]
    fixup  -> verify

    close_task -> check_remaining

    check_remaining -> pick_task [label="MORE", condition="preferred_label=MORE", loop_restart=true]
    check_remaining -> done      [label="DONE", condition="preferred_label=DONE"]
}

```
